coredata.dc:title: Algae derived carbon dots and its polymeric composites for white light emission
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Enriquez, B.U. Mendoza
Rangel, Miriam
Kumar, Y.
Khandual, S.
Agarwal, V.
coredata.dc:description:
Nanomaterials with multicolor emission have been of great interest due to their versatility. Recently, environmentally friendly carbon dots (CDs) with excitation wavelength/solvent/dopant dependent emission have been used for designing different types of white light emitting diodes (W-LED). In this work, single-green precursor (Arthrospira platensis) derived CDs from a one-step hydro/solvothermal synthesis, have been implemented in the elaboration of W-LEDs. The white emission of CDs was modulated by the solvent engineering and doping strategies. A CDs/PVA composite based W-LED with a color render index (CRI) upper to ∼90 %, Commission Internationale Eclairage (CIE) coordinates of (0.31, 0.33) and correlated color temperature (CCT) of
∼
6500 K is demonstrated. These findings suggest that white CDs derived from A. platensis possess promising potential in the field of optoelectronic devices, especially in the production of efficient and high-quality white illumination.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Multi-emission
White-LEDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para:
Schematic representation of solvent engineering strategy of algae derived CDs to modulate the white emission at
λ
ex=390 nm.

(Lower half) PL spectra (
λ e x = 400
n m
) and (upper half) images of the CDs depending on excitation wavelength in (a) different organic solvents and (b) solid state (CDs/PVA).
PVA films (shown as inset) photoluminescence spectrum at excitation of 400 nm and their CIE coordinates for CDs-water (a–b), CDs-ETOH (c–d), CDs-DMF (e–f), CDs-S (g–h) and CDs-FA (i–j).

a) PL spectra and b) CIE coordinates of PVA/CDs-S excited at different wavelengths 365–440 nm. PL Intensity vs wavelength diagram of the LED in operation (operation at 60 mA; 10W) for c) PVA/CDs-FA, d) PVA/CDs-ETOH, e) PVA-CDs-S and f) PVA-CDs-DMF. g) CIE coordinates of the different W-LEDs (CDs-DMF: (0. 30, 0.29), Tc 7500 K; CDs-EtOH: (0.32, 0. 31), CCT= 6000 K; CDs-S: (0.31, 0.33), CCT= 6500 K and CDs-FA: (0.34, 0. 34), CCT= 5000 K). h) CCT lighting scale; Image of pen caps for interpretation of the color appearance under i) sunlight, j) PVA/CDs-FA W-LED, and k) PVA/CDs-s W-LED.
Emission wavelengths corresponding to each type of CDs prepared with different solvents having Arthrospira platensis as precursor in this work.
Comparison of the present work with W-LEDs obtained using different other CDs reported in the literature.

row.entry: R. Kumari and S. K. Sahu 2022 [3]

head.ce:title: Algae derived carbon dots and its polymeric composites for white light emission

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center for Research and Assistance in Technology and Design of the State of Jalisco, AC (CIATEJ), Guadalajara 44680, Mexico

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Nanomaterials with multicolor emission have been of great interest due to their versatility. Recently, environmentally friendly carbon dots (CDs) with excitation wavelength/solvent/dopant dependent emission have been used for designing different types of white light emitting diodes (W-LED). In this work, single-green precursor (Arthrospira platensis) derived CDs from a one-step hydro/solvothermal synthesis, have been implemented in the elaboration of W-LEDs. The white emission of CDs was modulated by the solvent engineering and doping strategies. A CDs/PVA composite based W-LED with a color render index (CRI) upper to ∼90 %, Commission Internationale Eclairage (CIE) coordinates of (0.31, 0.33) and correlated color temperature (CCT) of
∼
6500 K is demonstrated. These findings suggest that white CDs derived from A. platensis possess promising potential in the field of optoelectronic devices, especially in the production of efficient and high-quality white illumination.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Solid-state white light emitting diodes, due to their great advantages in terms of energy efficiency, durability, fast response time and high luminous efficacy, represent an option to replace conventional light sources. Typically, W-LEDs are produced by combining blue chips and a yellow phosphor, or by joining near ultraviolet light chips with phosphors (red, green and blue) or coupling an UV chips with a white emissive phosphor [1]. However, the creation of white color faces the challenge of hue isolation due to the complementarity of blue and yellow colors, as well as the significant dependence on direct current. In addition, due to the absence of red color elements in the emitted light, LED devices typically have a low CRI and high CCT [2].

In general, quantum dots either from rare earths as Sm3+, Tb3+ and Dy3+ or some heavy metal ions have been used as starting materials to obtain white light. Nevertheless, such materials are rare, expensive, dangerous and involve complicated synthesis, making the large-scale production difficult [3]. Several strategies have been explored to synthesize CDs that emit light in different colors and longer wavelengths, such as varying the solvent used, adjusting the pH, introducing impurities of other elements, controlling the size and modifying their surface area [4]. For solid-state W-LEDs fabrication, the dispersion of CDs in polymeric matrices based, which helps to prevent luminescence extinction as a result of self-absorption induced by aggregation and complicated film-forming processes, has been reported [5]. CDs are carbon-based zero-dimensional materials which have been synthetized using biomass or even waste [6].

Spirulina (A. platensis) is one of the most abundant microalgae species due to the ease of growth in adverse conditions, making harvesting feasible. The spirulina biomass is also high in proteins, minerals like P, Mg, and K, and N [7], which is much higher than in other lignocellulosic sources [8]. The use of algae as a carbon source for CD synthesis creates a sustainable route for biomass recovery leading to a decrease in ecological impact. This spirulina composition helps to obtain a high quantum yield (QY), which is why CDs made from algae are used in many different applications [9].

In this work, CDs were synthesized from A . platensis by a single step solvothermal or hydrothermal method. CDs exhibited multicolor fluorescence in a broad-spectrum range blue-red leading to white emission with an excitation of 390 nm. The CDs emission was modulated in different solvents and introducing dopants in the CDs structure. W-LEDs were obtained by adding CDs into a polymeric matrix. Considering their multicolor emission behavior, it is feasible to use CDs from A. platensis as an excellent color converter.

The strain A. platensis 1Tex was collected from Texcoco lake, in the State of Mexico. The culture was maintained at 25 ± 2 °C with illumination of 3000 lux light [10]. N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF, 99.8 %), ethyl alcohol (ETOH, 95 %), sulphuric acid (H2SO4, 97.4 %) and formic acid (FA, 88 %) are from FERMOT. Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA, 95 %) is from Sigma Aldrich. Absorbance was measured using a Thermo Fisher scientific Evo-60 spectrophotometer, fluorescence spectra in a Varian Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrometer, FTIR spectra in a Varian 660-IR spectrophotometer, XPS data was acquired in a XPS/UPS/SXI-SEM, Scanning XPS Microprobe, model PHI 5000 Versa Probe II. The particle size by dynamic light scattering (DLS) in a Malvern Zetasizer Nano series and by FE-SEM in a Hitachi SU5000 equipment.

The quantum yield (QY) was determined using the relative approach, where Rhodamine 6G was used as reference (QY = 92 % in water). The integrated fluorescence intensity was plotted versus absorbance for different concentrations of CDs and reference (absorbance was maintained less than 0.1). The QY was calculated using the following equation:
Q Y =
Q Y
r
m s
m r
n s
2
n r 2
where m is the slope and n is the refractive index of the solvent (water = 1.33), the subscript r denotes the reference solution.

Polyvinyl alcohol (1 g) was dissolved in 10 ml of water at 80 °C and thermo-stirred for 1 h until obtain a clear solution. Then, the W-CDs were mixed with the PVA solution (volume ratio of 1:3) and stirred for 3 h. A glass mold was coated with the W-CDs/PVA solution and dried at room temperature, then the peeled off W-CDs/PVA film was placed on a CHIP LED UV (Fig. S1).

Highly emissive multicolor CDs were obtained with A. platensis as carbon source under different conditions (Fig. 1). The absorbance of CDs displays two main transitions, π→π∗ corresponding to C=C/C-C (∼260–280 nm) and n→π∗ (C=O at
∼
400 nm) (Fig. S2), a small shift of the transition bands to the right (with respect to CDs-water) has been attributed to the changes in the surface states (solvent effect) and heteroatom interaction [11]. XPS survey scan of CDs-water (Fig. S3a) reveals peaks at 283.9, 398.6 and 530.9 eV, corresponding to C1s (∼65 %), O1s (∼34 %) and N1s (∼1 %), respectively. FE-SEM (Fig. S3b) shows quasi-spherical shapes for CDs-water (∼80 nm), similar to size obtained by Dynamic Light Scattering (Fig. S3d). FTIR spectra of CDs/PVA films is shown in Fig. S3c. The relative QY of as-prepared CDs-water is ∼17 % (Fig. S3e).

The PL of CDs dispersed in different solvents, polar protic (H2O, H2O/FA, ETOH), polar aprotic (DMF) and polar protic with dopant (H2SO4), at
λ
ex of 400 nm is depicted in Fig. 2 a along with the photographic images of CDs at different excitation wavelength. The CDs exhibit excitation wavelength dependent PL emission characteristics (Fig. S4).

Synthesis under different conditions reveals the formation of CDs with emission in wide range of colors (from blue to red) (Fig. 2a). Under 400 nm excitation, CDs-water (green line) shows a cyan blue color with a unique emission band (494 nm), the rest of the CDs display three emission peaks, resulting in an emission that spans the entire spectrum and is perceived as white. The ultrabroad-band emission spectra could be originated from a broad distribution of the particle size [12,13]. For CDs-ETOH, blue shifted (CDs-water) fluorescence at 477 nm, along with the emerging bands between yellow (521 nm) and red (675 nm) (Summarized in Table S1) confirm the emergence of different surface states. The solvatocrhomic PL effect of CDs in polar protic solvents is attributed to the dipole-dipole interaction as well as the formation of hydrogen bonds, while in polar aprotic is caused by dipole-dipole interaction [14]. In case of CDs-FA the strong surface electron-withdrawing structure (due to the abundance of -COOH groups) contributes to the red shift of the emission spectrum [15]. Similar to our observations (two additional bands) for CDs-S, the incorporation of heteroatom has also been reported as contributing factor towards additional surface states [16].

The PL spectra in solid state form were obtained from flexible PVA/CDs films upon excitation with 400 nm along with their photographic images (Fig. 2b). The
λ
max of first emission band is ordered from less to higher wavelength for CDs-water, CDs-ETOH and CDs-DMF according to the decreasing value of their relative polarity (1, 0.654 and 0.386, respectively), while the second band is red shifted with an increase in the relative polarity (CDs-DMF, CDs-ETOH and CDs-water).

The color coordinates of CIE, in the cool white zone have been obtained from the PVA/CDs films (
λ
ex = 400 nm) (Fig. 3 ). PVA/CDs-S films are promising compounds for W-LED manufacturing, because they have a wide spectrum range (400–700 nm) and FWHM around 115 nm.

With an increase in excitation wavelength (from 365 to 430 nm), the PL emission spectra and the chromaticity diagram of PVA/CDs-S film reveal the shifting of emission from cold to warm white zone (Fig. 4 ab). W-LEDs (Fig. 4c–f), obtained using CDs-PVA composite solution over a commercial 400 nm InGaN UV chip (10 W, 0.95 mA), several layers of PVA/CDs were deposited on the chip and dried at room temperature (Fig. S5). W-LEDs showed a fairly wide visible spectra and CRI of ∼90 %. The CIE coordinates and CCT were also estimated. The presented parameters are comparable to the reported articles on this topic [17,18]. Fig. 4g summarize the CIE coordinates of the different proposed W-LEDs. Then, some different colored pen caps are used to verify the color rendering property (Fig. 4h). Their apparent colors are almost exactly the same under the W-LEDs and sunlight, demonstrating the high color rendering of the W-LEDs, Table S3 compares the luminous efficiency obtained for each of the W-LEDs. Photostability of CDs/PVA films was evaluated by measuring the initial PL of the film and irradiating continuously with a UV lamp during a period of 5 h, showing a degradation of 18 % (Fig. S6).

As compared to the published literature on CDs based W-LEDs (Table 2 ), in the present work simple one step processed biomass derived CDs have been demonstrated to produce white light emission with a great potential as environment friendly and scalable outdoor light emitting diodes.

In summary, CDs as luminescent nanomaterial are promising in the field of LEDs due to their low toxicity compared with classical materials, its low-cost and their tunable luminescence. Extendible to any type of algae, the proposed A. platensis derived CDs exhibit solvatochromic and excitation-dependent luminescent properties with wide band emission of tunable colors from 400 to 615 nm. Obtention of white light emission has been demonstrated using four different simple one-step methods involving either solvent engineering or sulphur dopant strategies. As a function of excitation wavelength, the PVA-CDs films can be easily formed to fabricate white-LEDs with CIE coordinates from cool white to warm white light emission. Applicable to toxic highly proliferating algae species, the efficacy of the proposed multifunctional nanomaterial will establish environmentally benign, sustainable and cost-effective approach for the fabrication of white LEDs and multicolor displays.

B.U. Mendoza Enriquez: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation. Miriam Rangel: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Y. Kumar: Supervision. S. Khandual: Resources. V. Agarwal: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: BUME (CVU:860502) and MR(CVU:592668) acknowledge CONAHCYT for doctoral and postdoctoral scholarships, respectively.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: “One-Step synthesis of white-light-emitting carbon dots for white LEDs with a high color rendering index of 97,”
Adv. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Yan et al., “One-Step Synthesis of White-Light-Emitting Carbon Dots for White LEDs with a High Color Rendering Index of 97,” Advanced Science, vol. 10, no. 12, p. 2206386, Apr. 2023, doi: 10.1002/advs.202206386.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: “Polymer-Assisted self-assembly of multicolor carbon dots as solid-state phosphors for fabrication of warm, high-quality, and temperature-responsive white-light-emitting devices,” ACS appl. Mater. Interfaces

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Wang, T. Hu, Y. Chen, Y. Xu, and Q. Song, “Polymer-Assisted Self-Assembly of Multicolor Carbon Dots as Solid-State Phosphors for Fabrication of Warm, High-Quality, and Temperature-Responsive White-Light-Emitting Devices,” ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces, vol. 11, no. 25, pp. 22332-22338, Jun. 2019, doi: 10.1021/acsami.9b04345.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Kumari and S. K. sb:title.sb:maintitle: “Preparation of N-doped carbon dots based on starch and their application in white LED,” Optical Materials,

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Liu, J. Zheng, Y. Yang, Y. Chen, and X. Liu, “Preparation of N-doped carbon dots based on starch and their application in white LED,” Optical Materials, vol. 86, pp. 530-536, Dec. 2018, doi: 10.1016/j.optmat.2018.10.057.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: “A pH-controlled synthetic route to violet, green, and orange fluorescent carbon dots for multicolor light-emitting diodes,”
Chem. Eng. J.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Dai, X. Chen, N. Ouyang, and Y. Hu, “A pH-controlled synthetic route to violet, green, and orange fluorescent carbon dots for multicolor light-emitting diodes,” Chemical Engineering Journal, vol. 431, p. 134172, Mar. 2022, doi: 10.1016/j.cej.2021.134172.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: “White light emitting diode and anti-counterfeiting applications of microwave assisted synthesized green fluorescent carbon dots derived from waste curry leaves,”
Results in Optics

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Maruthupandi, P. Varatharajan, I. B. Shameem Banu, M. Hafiz Mamat, and N. Vasimalai, “White light emitting diode and anti-counterfeiting applications of microwave assisted synthesized green fluorescent carbon dots derived from waste curry leaves,” Results in Optics, vol. 8, p. 100249, Aug. 2022, doi: 10.1016/j.rio.2022.100249.
